summary:
Simón Bolívar's military and political campaigns between 1810 and 1830 represent one of the most remarkable feats of leadership in modern history. Born in Caracas in 1783 to a wealthy Creole family, Bolívar became the central figure in the liberation of northern and western South America from Spanish colonial rule, earning the title El Libertador — The Liberator — that his contemporaries and posterity have confirmed.

Bolívar's path to victory was not straightforward. His early campaigns in Venezuela ended in failure and exile, and he was forced to relaunch the independence movement multiple times. His brilliance lay not only in military tactics but in political vision and sheer persistence. His Angostura Address of 1819, delivered to the Congress of Venezuela, outlined a republican constitutional framework that blended Enlightenment principles with the realities of Spanish American society. In the same year, he led a daring crossing of the Andes through the flooded llanos and up into the high passes — a campaign often compared to Hannibal's crossing of the Alps — to achieve a crushing surprise victory at the Battle of Boyacá, securing the independence of New Granada (present-day Colombia).

Bolívar went on to liberate Venezuela (again), Ecuador, Peru, and the territory that would bear his name — Bolivia — between 1821 and 1825. He briefly served as president of Gran Colombia, an ambitious union of present-day Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama that he hoped would anchor a broader Spanish American confederation. His vision was ultimately frustrated by regional rivalries and the resistance of local elites who preferred smaller, more manageable national units. Gran Colombia dissolved after his death in 1830, and Bolívar died the same year at the age of 47, reportedly expressing disillusionment about whether Latin American independence had fulfilled its promise.

Bolívar remains the preeminent political symbol across much of Latin America. His portrait adorns currencies, his name graces plazas, cities, and an entire country, and his writings continue to be studied as foundational texts of Latin American political thought. His legacy is contested — admired as a liberator and visionary, criticized for authoritarian tendencies in his later years — but his historical importance within the [[event:2526|Latin American independence movements]] is unquestioned.
